html:
TaskUs, Inc. (TASK)

TaskUs is no longer the net-cash, buyback-heavy setup described in older drafts. The better thesis is more interesting: it is a levered, hated, post-dividend equity stub attached to a still-profitable AI operations business that the market is valuing like a melting call center.

At $5.57, TASK has roughly a $510 million market cap and about $850 million to $900 million of enterprise value after the March 2026 debt recap. Management guided 2026 revenue to $1.21 billion to $1.24 billion, adjusted EBITDA margin to about 19%, and adjusted free cash flow to $105 million to $115 million. That is about 4.6x equity value to 2026 adjusted FCF, roughly 4x forward earnings, and under 4x EV/EBITDA. This is cheap enough that the thesis does not require heroic growth. It requires the business to not be dying.

The market’s label is simple: BPO plus AI automation risk plus Meta concentration. That is partly true. Meta was 26% of 2025 revenue and the largest client was still 24% of Q1 2026 revenue. Management openly said Trust & Safety revenue should decline year over year starting in Q2 because AI automation is reducing work at the largest client. That is the real bear case, and it is why the stock is near lows.

But the offset is visible in the same quarter. Q1 revenue grew 10.3% to $306.3 million. Excluding the largest client, revenue grew 13.5%. Clients 2 through 20 grew more than 20%, and the top 10 excluding the largest client grew over 30%. AI Services grew 36.1% to $61.9 million, now about one-fifth of company revenue, and more than 40% of Q1 signings were in AI Services. Management also said autonomous vehicle, robotics, and physical AI revenue should more than triple in 2026. This is not a legacy vendor hoping AI goes away. It is being paid both to automate legacy workflows and to supply human-in-the-loop data, model evaluation, remote assistance, mapping, safety, and operations for AI systems.

The variant perception is that TaskUs is not simply losing seats to AI agents. It is moving from labor arbitrage to AI-enabled operations. In the old model, a streaming client pays TaskUs for support agents. In the new model, TaskUs helps deploy agents, resells AI tools, wraps human escalation into the workflow, and eventually prices by outcome. That can pressure near-term margins because onshore AI work is lower margin and implementation-heavy, but if TaskUs controls the workflow it can capture part of the automation savings instead of merely losing headcount revenue.

The post-dividend capital structure is the torque. TaskUs paid a $3.65 special dividend, about $333 million total, funded with a new $500 million term loan and cash. The term loan matures in 2031 and bears SOFR plus 2.75%. Net leverage was under 1.4x adjusted EBITDA at Q1, so this is not distressed leverage, but it does remove the old net-cash downside floor. Equity holders now own a small market-cap claim on a larger enterprise. If EBITDA stays around $230 million and the market moves from panic multiples to 5.5x EV/EBITDA, the stock can double. If the market decides AI Services is a real growth segment and values TASK at 7x to 8x EBITDA, the equity can triple.

There is also a governance and perception angle. Blackstone and the co-founders tried to take TaskUs private at $16.50 per share in 2025 before the deal was terminated after the shareholder vote process. After the $3.65 dividend, that rough economic reference point is still far above today’s price. Activist Think Investments argued during the deal fight that $16.50 materially undervalued TASK and that a fair peer framework was closer to 8x to 12x 2026 EBITDA. I would not underwrite that full activist case, but it matters that knowledgeable insiders and investors were willing to argue about values far above the current quote.

The retail context is also unusually messy. A lot of recent discussion is about why the share price “collapsed” after the special dividend, plus lingering class-action settlement chatter, rather than the actual post-recap forward valuation. That kind of confusion can keep a small-cap stock mispriced, especially when the float is limited, analyst coverage is thin, and the headline story is “AI kills call centers.”

The risks are not cosmetic. If Meta’s automation wave becomes a faster, broader platform-wide reduction across Trust & Safety, revenue could decline before AI Services is large enough to offset it. Top 10 concentration rose to 63% in Q1, so diversification is still not good enough. Onshore AI delivery and wage increases are already pressuring margins. The CFO transition adds execution noise. The debt-funded dividend was shareholder-friendly in one sense, but it also made the equity more fragile. This is no longer a balance-sheet protected compounder. It is a cheap, levered re-rating candidate.

My base case is that 2026 is the trough narrative year. Trust & Safety declines, but DCX grows mid-to-high single digits, AI Services compounds above 25%, and physical AI/robotics becomes large enough to change the conversation. Revenue grows low-to-mid single digits, EBITDA stays around $230 million to $245 million, adjusted FCF holds near $110 million to $125 million, and leverage slowly falls. A 5.5x to 6x EBITDA multiple is not demanding for that profile.

Bear case: $3.25, about 42% downside from $5.57. Meta and other social clients automate faster than expected, revenue declines, EBITDA falls toward $175 million to $185 million, FCF drops below $75 million, and the market values the company at only 3.5x EBITDA less net debt.

Base case: $11.50, about 106% upside. 2026 guidance proves conservative, AI Services offsets Trust & Safety pressure, adjusted EBITDA holds near $235 million, adjusted FCF runs above $110 million, and the stock re-rates to about 5.5x EV/EBITDA.

Bull case: $20.00, about 259% upside. AI Services and physical AI become the dominant story, revenue growth reaccelerates toward high single digits or low double digits in 2027, EBITDA approaches $275 million to $300 million, FCF rises toward $150 million, and the market assigns 7x to 8x EBITDA to a founder-led AI operations platform rather than a dying BPO.
